author: @DMonZ1988
text:
very cool, but i really don't get it! so you have a convex lens catching the sounds from around the room, passing them through the CO2, moving the convex lens which focuses the sound into your ear?
why do you need two lenses at all and what does the CO2 do? i could imagine one convex lens catching the sound of a focused target and a second one, also convex, focusing it into your ear, basically giving you a big ear without using a giant cone, but if you're 'catching' sounds with a concave diaphram then couldn't it just be the back side of the same lens that then focuses the sound into your ear?
does the higher density gas transmit the sound waves better?

another solution that i think might work pretty well is if you actuate a sound lens directly with your painted speaker method shown recently, or with a more conventional transducer obviously.

i'm having ideas as i write this. i imagine creating a magnetic field to actuate a fairly large lens would also require a huge magnet, and with the coil panited onto the lens diaphram, the excited wire would be at different distances from the static magnet, and quickly really far away as you move out from the center.

but you can get flexible magnetic sheets. they aren't extremely magnetic, but i've seen them used to attach flexible build plates onto FDM 3d printers.
maybe you could cut one up to line a second, identical 'dish' placed as close as possible to the active diaphram lens as possible, so you can actuate a concave diaphram equally and effectively across its whole surface via a speaker coil panted onto it! so, it wouldn't be actuated linearly but rather into its focal point.

hope that makes sense.

EDIT: just went back a few videos and see that you actually thought about a bunch of this already, i'd only seen the painted and paper speaker experiments bfore this. buuuuut, combining a few of these concepts, i think you could actively drive a sound lens for a very directional speaker. i've made tech ingredients' panel speakers before but they aren't especially diectional, which is a very interesting feature, as you've here.

author: @petergambier
text:
What an amazing idea, as usual thanks Robert.

This got me thinking about a program where the security forces used a sound cannon to keep people back, (the Israeli military have perfected) apparently it's so powerful that they knocked a double decker bus over with one.

Any chance you could rig up something for a miniature home firing range using sticky-back plastic and binder twine to target plastic bottles and cans please?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I have been thinking about it lol

replies.author: @DoNotPushHere
replies.text: May I suggest a ring vortex launcher for that same firing range? I used to fire tiny vortexes to tje curtains and you could clearly see the hitting point on the hanging fabric :)

replies.author: @petergambier
replies.text:
@DoNotPushHere, I have a plastic toy that fires foam rings but don't want to mark the curtains, my woman would have my balls for next years Christmas decorations.

The ring vortex launcher proved to be too dodgy for non-lethal crowd control because if it was used to mark certain protesters with dye or chemicals it would also cover people under it's flight path. There's also a link to a page on a Hail cannon (shock wave generator) which I had also never heard about yet they have been around for over a century!

Some foreign security forces add a smelly chemical to the water which, like digestate from biodigester's is super-smelly and sticks to everything.

The nastiest control method has to be using microwaves to target people and is very effective at crowd dispersal because when you are within range your skin starts to heat up and it makes you feel uncomfortable and you really don't want to be there.

replies.author: @DoNotPushHere
replies.text:
@petergambier foam rings? I thought it was just smoke!? And nevertheless I thought you could just launch ye olde invisible air to avoid such a level of (involuntary) commitment in next year's decorations.

Do you know the name of the foam toy?

replies.author: @petergambier
replies.text: @DoNotPushHere, yes, it's the Executoy Stress Shooter. Article:57/9300, originally bought in 1999, and powered with 2 batteries, It fires up to 20 feet and has a magazine that holds 10 (you get 20 disks) and makes great noises when you pull the trigger. Happy new year from England.

author: @mjolnirswrath23
text:
Decibels do not destroy hearing distortion destroys hearing and causes ear fatigue my father invented a spherical speaker enclosure with a parabolic reflector dish made of fiberglass and had several us patents on it that reduced ear fatigue by 86%. As per Princeton audio labs research and established over a conventional box speaker which is more like furniture sound waves around why are people putting square pegs in round holes and calling this engineering?

The result was that he was able to do produce 246 DB with no cavitation of the sound waves which you use to push a turbine playing hotel California his input was a 12 volt car battery hooked to an amp to a sine wave generator to an oscilloscope and then afterwards he was running a generator using the sound waves in a Venturi system to push the turbine which was then running a generator which was connected to a 220 amp arc welder again his input was a 12 volt car battery his calculations was around 1185 feet per second pressure wave and in comparison Steam from a nuclear power generator pushes around 860 ft per second he called it project Jericho he taught me how to build it I know exactly how it works how it functions as I to see sound waves like you would see a mirage effect like he did something genetic who knows

author: @colleenforrest7936
text:
Those should be parabolic lenses, right? So the shallower the lenses the farther away the focal point should be.  Does refraction in the co2 change the natural parabolic focal point of  the back lense?

Would using vinegar and baking soda to make your co2 gas work? Suppose there was an outside chamber to make the co2 and a tube to feed it into the lens (co2 falls so the chamber should be above the lense and the tube entering at the top of the lense) and another hole at the top to let the air out, then close both ends when the lenses is full (maybe a match goes out at the output hole or something).

Would there be enough pressure in the co2 for the lensing effect to work?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I am sorry colleen you are asking questions I don't know the answer to yet

replies.author: @DaveJSmith
replies.text: Hi there, no they should be as Rob made them. Parabolas are used to reflect rather than 'lens' waves such as a parabolic mirror. This effect would be used in directional microphones for example. Lensing is a consequence of the speed of the wave changing as it crosses some sort of boundary (air and glass for light for example).

author: @manla8397
text:
Can you make concave lens with helium?  That would be an incredible experiment for students to demonstrate the nature of the lens converging/diverting depends on the both shapes and refractive index.  Not just the shapes.  Many students automatically will think a convex lens certainly a convergent.   A helium filled concave lens will certainly clear this misconception.   The technical difficulty will be how to retain helium long enough in your lens without letting it escapes. 

One more suggestion, when you refer to an old video, it will be good to add a link to your description so the viewers can easily found it.  

Thanks again for the great video and idea.

replies.author: @paulmaydaynight9925
replies.text: "Can you make concave lens with helium" sure just make the frame a cut down bellows filled with sealed helium with the added side benefit the device becomes a variable focal point by simple linear motion.

author: @ZsOtherBrother
text:
Awesome as usual :)
Is there a benefit to using CO2 rather than water in the lens?
Or in other words, is there a reason not to use a water lens to focus sound?
My first thought was that obviously sound travels in water, but maybe water reflects sound to such an extent that too little of it makes its way into the lens... or maybe the sound equivalent of refractive index of water vs. air prevents the sound from exiting the lens on the other side?!?
I'd love to hear what your physicist brother has to say about that.
Thanks

A small correction: "... in video 1834..." (02:06) should be "1384" ("How to make lenses...").

author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
text: also I wonder what the average speed of room temp CO2 is, compared to normal atmospheric gases which are supposedly around 80% nitrogen and 20% oxygen with those taking a number hit for the percentages of various other gases like argon, CO2 and such, mixed into them, and apparently the sound speed is an indicator of how fast the average atmospheric gas particle is moving, ie if the particles move slower then sound will travel slower. faster, and it causes the sound to travel faster, though this is with the obvious nature of some gas particles are moving super slow and others are moving much faster, but it all averages out in the end, or so I am told.

The other portion could possibly be done by micro cone shaping so phonons can collide increasing their energy quantity by combining at the points, to give the energy to jump to a photon of IR radiation level even when individually the thermal waves are not enough alone.
